coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of dihydropyrimidinone and dihydropyridine derivatives by a GQDs-based magnetically nanocatalyst under solvent-free conditions
coredata.dc:creator: Najjar, Morvarid
Nasseri, Mohammad Ali
Darroudi, Majid
Allahresani, Ali
coredata.dc:description: A novel and heterogeneous catalyst were prepared of magnetic nanoparticles Fe3O4 coated with silicate modified with graphene quantum dot-copper (GQD/Cu) and used as an efficient catalyst in the multicomponent reactions of Biginelli and Hantzsch. The nanocatalyst was examined by Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR), thermogravimetric-differential thermal, X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM), energy‐dispersive X‐ray (EDX), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) analysis. A wide range of 3, 4-dihydro pyrimidine-2-(1 H)-one (DHPM) and 1, 4-dihydropyridine (DHP) derivatives were synthesized with good-to-excellent efficiencies in the presence of the catalyst using a combination of aldehydes, 1, 3-dicarbonyls, and urea/ammonium acetate. The catalyst was taken out and recovered from the reaction mixture by an external magnetic field and reused after six runs with minimal activity loss.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel and heterogeneous catalyst were prepared of magnetic nanoparticles Fe3O4 coated with silicate modified with graphene quantum dot-copper (GQD/Cu) and used as an efficient catalyst in the multicomponent reactions of Biginelli and Hantzsch. The nanocatalyst was examined by Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR), thermogravimetric-differential thermal, X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM), energy‐dispersive X‐ray (EDX), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) analysis. A wide range of 3, 4-dihydro pyrimidine-2-(1 H)-one (DHPM) and 1, 4-dihydropyridine (DHP) derivatives were synthesized with good-to-excellent efficiencies in the presence of the catalyst using a combination of aldehydes, 1, 3-dicarbonyls, and urea/ammonium acetate. The catalyst was taken out and recovered from the reaction mixture by an external magnetic field and reused after six runs with minimal activity loss.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: The multicomponent reaction (MCR) contains at least three reactants which are combined in one reaction vessel and during one step to form a new molecule so that each reactant forms part of it [1,2]. MCR toward multi-step reaction has advantages such as reducing the number of reaction steps, atom economy, and time savings [3]. Economic and environmental advantages have increased the importance of these strategies over time [4].

MCRs Biginelli and Hantzsch are the most useful and practical reactions of this class due to the production of nitrogen heterocycles [5,6]. Initially, the Hantzsch reaction was reported in 1882 when acetoacetic ester and aldehyde, and ammonia were heated to synthesize DHPs [7–10]. DHP derivatives have attracted much attention for medicinal and therapeutic properties, including neuroprotective, glycoprotein inhibitors, vasodilators [11], antibacterial, and anticancer [12].

About a decade after the synthesis of DHPs, Pietro Biginelli, an Italian chemist, synthesized the new molecule DHPM, by combining three primary ingredients, including aldehyde, ethyl acetoacetate, and urea, using an acid catalyst, while its byproduct was similar to DHPs [13,14].

DHPM derivatives show a wide range of important medicinal and biological properties, such as antiseptic [15], antihypertensive [16], anticancer [17], and calcium channel modulators [18]. The biological and industrial importance of DHP and DHPM derivatives have developed the catalysts and techniques for their synthesis, such as sulfuric acid catalysts [19], Ni nanoparticles (NPs) catalysts [20], and magnetic catalysts [21], the use of solar thermal energy [22], ionic liquid [23], polymers [24], and microwave radiation [25]. In recent decades, catalytic processes have become more critical in the use of inexpensive reagents to produce cleaner and more expensive products [26]. With the development of heterogeneous catalysts, nano generations of these catalysts, have attracted much attention [21].

Magnetic NPs such as Fe3O4 has attracted the attention of chemical researchers and they have a special place in sustainable and clean chemistry, due to their easy separation from the reaction medium, high surface-to-volume ratio, low toxicity, and easy surface modification [27,28]. Nevertheless, Fe3O4 NPs lose their magnetic properties due to limitations such as high reactivity, rapid aggregation, and high sensitivity to moisture and air [29,30]. Coating with organic or mineral materials is the most common surface modification of Fe3O4 NPs. SiO2 is one of the best candidates for the formation of iron-oxide NPs with core-shell structures, due to its advantages such as physicochemical durability, high thermal stability, and unique surface properties [31–34]. Since the Fe3O4@SiO2 core-shell is a heterogeneous catalyst, the ratio of activity to selectivity decreases with increasing mass diffusion to the catalyst sites [35,36]. This restriction was remedied by the use of organic compounds or the formation of organic-inorganic bonds through the attachment of organic-metal parts to mobile chains connected to the core-shell surface [37,38]. GQDs are carbon nanosheets whose thickness is less than 10 nm and their other dimensions are less than 100 nm [39]. GQDs have significant properties such as low toxicity, excellent dispersion in water, robust chemical inertness, fluorescence activity, and stronger surface bonding than other carbon nanomaterials and many surface groups (-COOH, -OH, -CO, and Epoxy) [40–45]. In recent years, GQDs have had extensive achievements in applications such as sensors [46], catalysts [47–51], drug delivery [52], and bioimaging [53].

In this paper, we modified the Fe3O4@SiO2 core-shell surface by GQDs/Cu to prepare a green and developed copper heterogeneous catalyst (Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II)). Therefore, combining the advantages of a solid magnetic platform and GQDs/Cu, the synthesis of a new organic-inorganic hybrid Cu-catalyst has been reported that can be effective for the conversion of various functional groups. To investigate the efficiency and activity of this catalyst, we targeted the Biginelli and Hantzsch MCR reactions. Using the prepared catalyst of Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II), the desired products were synthesized with very good to excellent yields in solvent-free conditions, which we will discuss further.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Fe3O4@SiO2 functionalized with epichlorohydrin (Fe3O4 @SiO2-Epoxide)
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@SiO2 NPs were synthesized via the previously described procedure [54]. To prepare of Fe3O4 @SiO2-Epoxide, 1 g Fe3O4@SiO2 was dispersed in toluene (12.5 mL) and 5 mmol of epichlorohydrin (ECH) and 10 mmol of Et3N were added to the dispersed mixture. Then, the reaction mixture was refluxed by stirring under an Ar atmosphere for 42 h. The product was separated by an external magnet and washed with ethanol before drying at 60 ℃ in a vacuum oven.

The 2 g citric acid was put into the crucible and heated at a heating mantle (200 ℃). After 5 min, the citric acid was liquefied. The colorless liquid was changed to a pale yellow gel after 25 min. The gel was added dropwise to 10% NaOH solution (100 mL) under vigorous stirring. The mixture was neutralized (pH=7) with a NaOH solution (10%) and the GQDs solution was obtained [55]. 0.2 g Cu(OAc)2·H2O was added to the GQDs solution. The mixture was stirred at 60 ℃ for 4 h and dried in the oven.

The 0.4 g of Fe3O4@SiO2-Epoxide was dispersed in 20 mL of EtOH. Then 0.04 g GQDs/Cu was added to the mixture and refluxed with stirring for 12 h. The product, Fe3O4 @SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II), was separated by an external magnet and washed with ethanol before drying in the vacuum oven. Scheme 1 shows the complete route for the synthesis of the Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II).

ce:section.ce:section-title: General method for the preparation of DHPM derivatives from Biginelli reaction using the Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II)
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of aromatic aldehyde (1.0 mmol), urea (1.2 mmol), ethyl acetoacetate (1.0 mmol), and the Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II) catalyst (0.013 mol% or 0.02 g) was stirred in a solvent-free medium in an oil bath under reflux at 110 °C for 120 min. The progress of the reaction was continuously monitored by TLC. In the following, 5 mL of dichloromethane (CH2Cl2) was added to the mixture and stirred for 5 min. The catalyst was collected by an external magnet, washed several times with ethyl acetate (EtOAc) and EtOH, and dried in an oven (60 ℃) to be reused in the next run. The product was extracted with CH2Cl2 and the anhydrous sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) was used to dry the organic layer. The pure coupling product was procured using silica gel plate chromatography. The obtained pure products were characterized by comparing their melting points with the reported samples and by NMR analyses their data are available in the supplementary information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General method for the preparation of DHP Derivatives (polyhydroquinolines (PHQs) and hexahydroquinolines (HHQs)) from Hantzsch reaction catalyzed using Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II) catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of ammonium acetate (1.5 mmol), ethyl acetoacetate (2.0 mmol), aromatic aldehyde (1.0 mmol), and Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II) catalyst (0.013 mol% or 0.02 g) was stirred in a solvent-free medium in an oil bath under reflux at 90 °C for 30 min. The progress of the reaction was completely monitored by TLC. Then, 5 mL of EtOAc was added to the mixture and stirred for 5 min. The catalyst was collected from the reaction mixture using an external magnet, washed several times with EtOAc and EtOH, and dried in a vacuum oven (60 ℃) to be reused in the next run. The mixture was extracted with chloroform (CHCl3) and the anhydrous Na2SO4 was used to dry the organic layer. The pure coupling product was obtained using silica gel plate chromatography. The obtained pure products were characterized by comparing their melting points with the reported samples and by NMR analyses their data are available in the supplementary information.

FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2, Fe3O4@SiO2-Epoxide, and Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II) represents in Fig. 1. In Fig. 1A, the stretching vibration is assigned to Fe–O bond of Fe3O4 NPs at 568 cm−1 [56]. The presence of three strong peaks in the spectrum of Fe3O4@SiO2 at 583, 1076, and 3405 cm−1 were related to Fe–O, Si–O–Si, and –OH stretching vibrations bands, respectively, which confirms the coating of Fe3O4 NPs by SiO2 layers (Fig. 1B) [57]. Fig. 1C exhibits Fe–O, Si–O–Si, epoxide ring, C–O, CH2, and O-H stretching vibrations at 586, 794, 1087, 1393, 2867, and 3433 cm−1, respectively for the Fe3O4@SiO2-Epoxide spectrum, that the additional peak at 794 cm−1 strongly confirms the preparation of Fe3O4@SiO2-Epoxide from the reaction of Fe3O4 @SiO2 with ECH [58]. Moreover, other stretching vibrations of the epoxide ring are covered by other absorption bonds in the spectrum [59]. Fig. 1D shows the strong peaks at 587, 1091, 1380, 2870, and 3430 cm−1 corresponding to Fe–O, Si–O–Si, C–O, CH2, and O-H stretching vibrations in the Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II). The additional peaks at 1593, and 1650 cm−1 is corresponding to the stretching vibrations of CC, and CO in the aromatic ring from GQDs [60].

The thermal behavior of the catalyst shows by TGA ( Fig. 2a). Thermal decomposition takes place in one step up to about 500 ℃ with a gentle slope corresponding to a weight loss of 18.7%. This weight loss revealed the removal of the GQDs loaded on the magnetic nanoparticle. After these temperatures, the catalyst showed no significant weight loss until temperatures of 1000 ℃.

The crystalline structures of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2, and Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II) NPs were investigated by XRD analysis (Fig. 2b). The characteristic peaks at 2θ = 30.52 ̊, 36.02 ̊, 43.57 ̊, 53.97 ̊, 57.77 ̊, and 63.27 ̊ for pure Fe3O4 NPs which are remarked respectively by their indices (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), (440) planes. The Fe3O4 NPs have a cubic inverse spinel structure with a highly crystalline in agreement with the crystal structure of Fe3O4 NPs characterized by XRD (JCPDS card no. 19–629). Also, these peaks were examined for Fe3O4@SiO2 and Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II) NPs, which confirmed the inverse spinel structure of Fe3O4 in these NPs (Fig. 2bA and B). The intensity of the diffraction peaks in the functionalized magnetic NPs reduced in comparison with pure Fe3O4 NPs, probably due to amorphous silica and GQDs/Cu complex coating. Therefore, this result indicates that the crystal structure of Fe3O4 remains intact during silica coating and surface functionalization. The XRD patterns of Fe3O4@SiO2 and Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II) present a peak at a low diffraction angle (10−25) which has been successfully synthesized the coated amorphous silica on Fe3O4 NPs. For Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II), GQDs presented a broad peak in the peak range of amorphous silica (20 ̊). Therefore, the broadening and reduction of the peak corresponding to silica indicates a successful surface modification of the Fe3O4@SiO2 core-shell NPs.

The VSM analysis characterizes the magnetic behavior of Fe3O4, Fe3O4 @SiO2, and Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II) NPs. As shown in Fig. 2c, these NPs had a high magnetic permeability, which was sufficient to collect by an external magnet. According to Fig. 2c, the residual magnetization for all NPs was zero. The saturation magnetization measurements were 65.4, 56.8, and 52.8 emu/g for the Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2, and Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II), respectively. The results show that the magnetization of Fe3O4 NPs was decreased by coating the silica shell or Cu (II) complex on its surface significantly. However, the catalyst showed superparamagnetic characteristics with a high magnetization value, which can be removed by an external magnetic field from the reaction mixture. EDX analysis was performed to determine the present elements in the catalyst and given in Fig. 2d. All elements identified in the complex including iron (22.48%), oxygen (43.23%), silicon (6.31%), carbon (27.52%), and copper (0.42%). The size and morphology of Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II) were determined by TEM and FE-SEM analyses. According to the TEM image ( Fig. 3a), the obtained NPs had a spherical shape with an average size of 25 nm. The FE-SEM image (Fig. 3b) also confirmed that the NPs had a spherical morphology and are well dispersed.

Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II) as catalyst was investigated in the synthesis of DHPM derivatives, screening of reaction conditions such as solvent, temperature, and dose of catalyst in the selective reaction between benzaldehyde and urea and ethyl acetoacetate as a model reaction. The selected premium conditions were achieved in the presence of 0.01 g (0.0065 mol%) of the catalyst in different solvents at 90 ℃ in the model reaction, which obtained a yield of 85% of the product in 120 min ( Fig. 4a). Solvent-free medium and DMSO was selected as the most effective medium and other solvents had lower product yields. The solvent-free medium was selected as the optimal medium to apply the principles of green chemistry. For further study, the effect of temperature was explored on the reaction efficiency (Fig. 4b). The best reaction temperature was 110 °C which obtained the highest product yield (92%). The lower yields have resulted at other temperatures (60, 90 °C, and 120 °C). Then, the effect of different amounts of the catalyst was presented in Fig. 4c. The highest yield of product was obtained in 0.02 g (0.013 mol%) of the catalyst. In addition, the product yield was a trace in the absence of the catalyst. Therefore, increasing the catalyst amount did not have a positive effect on product yield, and even a decreasing trend was observed in efficiency [61].

Synthesis ethyl 6-methyl-2-oxo-4-phenyl-3, 4-dihydro-1 H-pyrimidine-5-carboxylatea.
fx1
.

The Biginelli reaction was performed for the various aromatic aldehydes with two types of 1, 3 dicarbonyl compounds, namely dimedone and ethyl acetoacetate, and Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II) catalyst (0.013 mol%) in a solvent-free medium at 110 °C ( Table 1). As shown in Table 1 (entry 1a-h), various aromatic aldehydes have a significant effect on product yield in reaction with ethyl acetoacetate. It was observed, that aldehydes with electron acceptor groups had higher product yield than electron donor groups. The electron acceptor group increases the electrophilic property of the carbonyl group which increases its reactivity to nucleophilic species [62]. In addition, synthesis of the DHPM different derivatives was performed for another 1,3 dicarbonyl, dimedone, with different aromatic aldehydes in the presence of Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II) catalyst (0.013 mol%) in a solvent-free medium at 110 °C (Table 1, entry 2a-g).

The reaction mechanism for the synthesis of DHPMs was investigated based on the previous mechanism proposed by Kappe [2]. First, the reaction is carried out through the nucleophilic addition of urea to the aldehyde, and an imine intermediate is formed. By coordinating Cu2+ with the imine nitrogen atom, the CN group is activated toward nucleophilic attack. On the other hand, the complexation-ketoester with Cu2+ enhances the nucleophilicity of α-carbonenolate, and the attack on the imine group can be performed without the intervention of the carbonyl group to form an open-chain intermediate ureide. Then, cyclization and dehydration would afford DHPMs [78] ( Scheme 2).

DHPs form a large family of medical compounds [79]. In the last years, many attempts have been performed to prepare these compounds due to their biological activity [80]. Quinolines are compounds that have a structural nucleus of DHPs and are very important because of their medicinal properties [81]. PHQs and HHQs are examples of quinolines that are synthesized using the Hantzsch reaction [82]. The synthesis of these compounds was performed in the presence of many metal catalysts as effective catalysts [83–86]. Therefore, the catalytic activity of Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II) was evaluated in the preparation of DHP derivatives. Therefore, the combination of benzaldehyde (1.0 mmol), ammonium acetate (1.5 mmol), and ethyl acetoacetate (2.0 mmol) was selected as the model reaction (synthesis diethyl 2,6-dimethyl-4-phenyl-1,4-dihydropyridine-3,5-dicarboxylate) using Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II) catalyst in a solvent-free medium. In addition, the effect of solvent and temperature parameters was screened for achieving higher product yields and the reaction progress was continuously monitored by TLC. The results are summarized in Fig. 5.

Synthesis diethyl 2,6-dimethyl-4-phenyl-1,4-dihydropyridine-3,5-dicarboxylate
fx4
.

Initially, the product yield in the model reaction was a trace in the absence of the catalyst for 30 min. As shown in Fig. 5a, to select the best solvent, the model reaction was performed in H2O+EtOH at 90 °C in the presence of the catalyst (0.013 mol%) in 65% yield. The product yield in the toluene solvent increased to 40%. Glycerol, DMF, CH3CN, and DMSO provided the product with yields of 50%, 72%, 82%, and 85%, respectively. The solvent-free medium was selected as the optimal medium with the highest product yield (86%). As shown in Fig. 5b, the best reaction temperature was 110 °C which obtained the highest product yield (90%). The fewer yields have resulted at lower and higher temperatures (60, 90, and 120 °C). Then, the various DHP derivatives (PHQs and HHQs) were synthesized in the presence of Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II) catalyst and the optimum conditions (
Tables 2 and 3). It was obtained from the results that the nature of the electron acceptor or donor group on aryl halides had no significant effect on the formation of DHP derivatives at a short reaction time [87].

The reusability of the catalyst has been a significant commercial and industrial advantage [107]. Therefore, the reusability of the catalyst was investigated over the model Biginelli reaction under optimal conditions and the catalyst was reused for six runs. As shown in Fig. 6, a limited reduction (only a 7% decrease) in catalyst activity was observed after the sixth cycle. On the other hand, to measure copper metal leaching, ICP analysis was performed from the residual solution after each cycle. The leaching amount after the sixth cycle was 1.8% which was negligible.

After the sixth run, to ensure the recovered catalyst structure, several analyses were performed to compare the recovered catalyst with the fresh catalyst ( Fig. 7). FT-IR analysis confirmed that the structure of the catalyst was as same as the fresh catalyst and the catalyst structure remained intact (Fig. 7a). The magnetic property decreases in the used catalyst that is caused by the leaching happening of the catalyst (Fig. 7b). Therefore, the fresh catalyst is more affected by the external magnet. The FESEM image of the recovered catalyst showed that the NPs were approximately spherical and no morphological changes were observed in the catalyst (Fig. 7c). The morphology and homogeneity of the catalyst were shown in the TEM image of the recovered catalyst (Fig. 7d).

A comparative study was carried out considering the catalytic activity of the catalyst with the reported catalysts in literature for the preparation of DHPM and DHP derivatives ( Table 4). Synthetic methods in this paper are superior to other methods in terms of performance and separation of catalyst from reaction environment and reaction time.

A probable mechanism is proposed for the synthesis of DHPs from various aldehydes and ethyl acetoacetate and ammonium acetate catalyzed by Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II). First, the carbonyl group of aldehyde is activated using Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II) catalyst to be attacked by the enol form of ethyl acetoacetate and the corresponding Kenovenagle intermediate is obtained. This intermediate is activated in the presence of Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II) to can be attacked by obtained enamine, from the reaction of NH3 and the second mole of ethyl acetoacetate, that the imine intermediate is produced. In the next step, with the activation of the remaining keto group in the intermediate using Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II), cyclization would afford by the amine group. Finally, by removing the third water molecule from the enamine part and dehydrogenation, the corresponding DHP is obtained [119,120] ( Scheme 3).

The model Biginelli reaction, to prove the unique catalytic activity of Fe3O4@SiO2-GQDs/Cu (II), was investigated in the absence of catalysts, Fe3O4 NPs, Fe3O4@SiO2, Fe3O4@SiO2 -Epoxide, GQDs, GQDs/Cu, and Cu(OAc)20.2 H2O. As shown in Table 5, the model reaction showed trace yield in the absence of catalyst and Cu(OAc)20.2 H2O (Table 5, entries 1 and 2). The Fe3O4 NPs increased efficiency by up to 10% (Table 5, entries 3). The Fe3O4 @SiO2 core-shell respectively showed 13% efficiency (Table 5, entries 4), and the presence of Hydroxyl functional groups on the core-shell surface increased the efficiency. Modification of the surface of the core-shell with epichlorohydrin (Fe3O4@SiO2 -Epoxide) increased its catalytic activity to 25% (Table 5, entry 5). With the use of GQD as a catalyst, the yield of the product reached 30% (Table 5, entry 6), which the apparent reasons could be the nanometer size (1–10 nm) and the presence of various functional groups (-OH and -COOH) on the surface of GQD. The most interesting results were observed using the GQD/Cu complex as a catalyst that achieved 65% efficiency of the product (Table 5, entry 7). The catalytic activity was increased by immobilizing QGD/Cu on the surface of Fe3O4@SiO2 -Epoxide (Table 5, entry 8). The results also show that the surface of Fe3O4@SiO2 -Epoxide is coated with GQD/Cu. The synthesized Catalyst achieved the highest yield (95%), which can be considered a result of the catalytic synergistic effect of these groups.

In this work, a heterogeneous magnetic catalyst was synthesized using copper supported on the GQDs as carbon-based support. The unique properties of the GQDs facilitate their surface modification by inorganic and organic agents. The GQDs have increased the possibility of effective interactions of organic compounds and formed DHPM and DHP variety derivatives with good efficiency due to preventing leaching and agglomeration of NPs, limiting the side reactions, and having several functional groups. Easy separation of this heterogeneous metallic catalyst as an impurity has added to its advantages for the synthesis of DHPM and DHP derivatives, which have biological and medical applications. The present method has advantages such as easy method, good product efficiency, easy catalytic reusability, and solvent-free conditions with economically advantageous without the use of corrosive liquid acids.
